pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP) THE SMOKING GUN: The combination of acute neuropsychiatric symptoms, severe autonomic dysfunction (hypertension/tachycardia alternating with hypotension), rapidly ascending polyneuropathy, and the burgundy-colored urine that darkens upon light exposure (due to porphobilinogen and porphyrins oxidizing to porphobilin) PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: AIP is an autosomal dominant metabolic disorder caused by a deficiency in the enzyme hydroxymethylbilane synthase (HMBS), also known as porphobilinogen deaminase (PBG-D). This deficiency impairs the heme biosynthesis pathway. When triggered (often by stress, fasting, hormones, or certain medications like barbiturates/sulfa drugs), the pathway shifts, leading to the massive accumulation of neurotoxic precursors: delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) and porphobilinogen (PBG). These accumulated precursors cause direct neurotoxicity, affecting both the central nervous system (psychosis, anxiety), peripheral nerves (pain, paralysis), and the autonomic nervous system (tachycardia, labile blood pressure, abdominal pain). TREATMENT: High-dose intravenous glucose (to suppress the enzyme synthesis) and, crucially, intravenous Hemin (Panhematin), which is exogenous heme that feedback-inhibits the initial enzyme, ALA synthase, thereby dramatically reducing the production of the toxic precursors ALA and PBG.
